rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JAMES J. & ROSELLA C. KENNEDY 1998 REVOCALBLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To Robert Kennedy?S Petition To Remove Trustee And For Redress Of Trustees Mismanagement And Misappropriation Of Trust Assets, As Amended
rulings.Rulings: This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Before the court is Respondent and Trustee James J. Kennedy III's demurrers to the Second, Third, and Fourth Causes of Action of Petitioner and Beneficiary Robert Kennedy's Amended Petition to Remove Trustee and for Redress of Trustee's Mismanagement and Misappropriation of Trust Assets. The demurrer is OVERRULED. The issue is whether assuming true the facts properly pled and facts properly noticed, do the challenged claims necessarily fail. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10(e); Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) "[I]f, on consideration of all facts stated, it appears that plaintiff is entitled to any relief against defendant, complaint will be held good, though facts may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with statement of other facts irrelevant to cause of action shown, or though plaintiff may demand relief to which he is not entitled under facts alleged." (Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236, quoting Matteson v. Wagoner (1905) 147 Cal. 739, 742.) Here, the challenged causes of action easily survive the demurrers. Petitioner alleges that Respondent, as Trustee, has breached his fiduciary duties to the beneficiaries, by using Trust property for his personal benefit and the benefit of his separate business; not charging his separate business market rates for the use of Trust assets; co-mingling his personal or business funds with Trust funds; using Trust funds for his own benefit and making disbarments of Trust funds to non-beneficiaries, and failing to account to the beneficiaries. The Petition alleges Respondent has breached his fiduciary duty by mismanaging or misappropriating Trust assets that were to flow (at least in part) to Petitioner as a beneficiary. On the face of the Petition, admitting the truth of all material facts properly pleaded, it appears that, if the allegations are true, Petitioner may be entitled to some kind of relief against Respondent, This is a sufficient showing to overcome Respondent's demurrer to the Second, Third, and Fourth causes of action on the basis of failure to state a cause of action. The prayer and the demurred causes of action do seek various forms of monetary damages, including punitive damages, but even if the relief prayed for exceeds what is warranted by the pleaded facts-and the court is making no such finding, that is not grounds for demurrer. (Garris v. Mitchell (1935) 7 Cal.App.2d 430, 436.) Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on September 9, 2020. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate.
